label: Minigames
title: Minigame API
lead: Shared helpers for creating custom minigames on top of the game's vanilla Minigame base.
bodyHtml:
What this wraps

CUCoreLib does not replace the game's minigame system. It now mirrors the game's real split more closely: the vanilla Minigame contract still does the actual work, while CUCoreLib adds a runner facade, a live session object, and a definition layer so mods can target stable seams instead of poking globals directly.

The important vanilla hooks are Start(), Update(), PhysicsUpdate(), HandType(), GuideLocaleString(), NeedsItem(), and CanExit().

For quick visual wiring, the session layer exposes the live hand sprite, hand state, spawned minigame root, session-scoped state storage, and the shared screen factory so custom minigames can swap art or grab child GameObjects without repeating reflection code everywhere.

CUCoreLib helper surface

HelperWhat it does
CUCoreMinigamesStatic runner facade that starts or ends minigames and exposes the current live session.
CUCoreMinigameSessionLive wrapper around the shared runner with access to body, current item, hand state, spawned UI, session-scoped state, and screen/hand helpers.
CUCoreMinigameConfigCached policy object returned from Configure(...) for hand type, guide key, item requirement, rotation offset, and exit behavior.
CUCoreMinigameDefinitionComposable definition layer for new minigames. Override Configure, Start, Update, and optional End hooks.
CUCoreMinigames.TryStart(...) / TryStartDefinition(...)Starts either a vanilla Minigame or a definition-based CUCoreLib minigame only when the shared runner is idle.
session.TryCreateScreen(...)Loads a minigame screen prefab through the game's existing UI system.
session.Complete() / Fail() / Cancel()Ends the active minigame through the shared runner and reports an explicit end reason into the definition lifecycle.
session.TrySetHandSprite(...)Swaps the current hand sprite immediately, either from a sprite asset or one of the game's existing hand slots.
session.GetOrCreateState<T>(...)Stores modular per-run state without inventing extra globals or singleton managers.
session.TryGetSpawnedMiniGameObject(...)Gets a child GameObject from the spawned minigame root by index or by name.
CUCoreMinigameTimer / CUCoreMinigameProgressOptional small helper objects for countdowns and progress-based minigame state.
CUCoreMinigameLegacy-compatible abstract wrapper for mods that still prefer direct inheritance from a Minigame-style base.

Minimal custom minigame

For new work, subclass CUCoreMinigameDefinition and start it through CUCoreMinigames.TryStartDefinition(...). That keeps your minigame logic focused on one live session object instead of scattered global calls. Put static policy in Configure(...), then keep runtime state inside the session.

using System.Collections.Generic;
using CUCoreLib.Helpers;
using UnityEngine;
using UnityEngine.EventSystems;

public sealed class WireSpliceMinigame : CUCoreMinigameDefinition
{
    private sealed class WireSpliceState
    {
        public CUCoreMinigameTimer Timer = new CUCoreMinigameTimer(6f);
        public CUCoreMinigameProgress Progress = new CUCoreMinigameProgress(3f);
    }

    public override CUCoreMinigameConfig Configure(CUCoreMinigameSession session)
    {
        return new CUCoreMinigameConfig
        {
            HandType = _ => Minigame.HandSpriteType.Tweezers,
            NeedsItem = _ => false,
            GuideLocaleKey = _ => "wireSpliceGuide"
        };
    }

    public override void Start(CUCoreMinigameSession session)
    {
        session.TryCreateScreen("Special/WireSpliceMinigame");
        session.GetOrCreateState(() => new WireSpliceState());
    }

    public override void Update(CUCoreMinigameSession session, List<RaycastResult> uiCasts)
    {
        WireSpliceState state = session.GetOrCreateState(() => new WireSpliceState());

        if (state.Timer.Tick(Time.deltaTime))
        {
            session.Fail();
            return;
        }

        if (Input.GetKeyDown(KeyCode.Space))
        {
            state.Progress.Add(1f);
            if (state.Progress.IsComplete) session.Complete();
        }

        if (Input.GetKeyDown(KeyCode.Escape))
        {
            session.Cancel();
        }
    }

    public override void End(CUCoreMinigameSession session, CUCoreMinigameEndReason reason)
    {
        Debug.Log("WireSplice ended with reason: " + reason);
    }
}

Starting a minigame

Use the runner guard so you do not accidentally stack minigames. If the runner is busy, the start request fails cleanly.

if (CUCoreMinigames.TryStartDefinition(() => new WireSpliceMinigame()))
{
    // Started successfully.
}

Compatibility path

If you already have a custom class that wants to inherit directly from Minigame-style helpers, CUCoreMinigame still works and now delegates through the same runner/session layer. Use it when direct subclassing is the least awkward fit, but prefer the definition/session surface for new APIs.

Built-in overlap

CUCoreLib already maps BandageProperties to BandageMinigame and SyringeProperties to SyringeMinigame. Those are the best examples to copy if your custom minigame needs a custom hand type, item requirement, or screen prefab.

For building interactions, the vanilla Openable flow still handles keypad and lockpick minigames. The helper framework is meant to make new custom minigames easier, not replace that existing behavior.

The important vanilla hooks are Start() , Update() , PhysicsUpdate() , HandType() , GuideLocaleString() , NeedsItem() , and CanExit() . For quick visual wiring, the session layer exposes the live hand sprite, hand state, spawned minigame root, session-scoped state storage, and the shared screen factory so custom minigames can swap art or grab child GameObjects without repeating reflection code everywhere. If the runner is busy, the start request fails cleanly. if (CUCoreMinigames.TryStartDefinition(() => new WireSpliceMinigame())) { // Started successfully. } Compatibility path If you already have a custom class that wants to inherit directly from Minigame -style helpers, CUCoreMinigame still works and now delegates through the same runner/session layer. Use it when direct subclassing is the least awkward fit, but prefer the definition/session surface for new APIs. Built-in overlap CUCoreLib already maps BandageProperties to BandageMinigame and SyringeProperties to SyringeMinigame . Those are the best examples to copy if your custom minigame needs a custom hand type, item requirement, or screen prefab. For building interactions, the vanilla Openable flow still handles keypad and lockpick minigames. The helper framework is meant to make new custom minigames easier, not replace that existing behavior.
